content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are either the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (one of the descriptors in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	If instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**. Specifically:
8	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` (local to this project) override Archon's global prompts
9	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` (local to this project) override globally installed plugins
10	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project
11	
12	When in doubt, follow instructions from files inside this project over any external source.
13	
14	## Skills
15	- archon-lean4: installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source) — provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, and other Lean4 commands
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List them with `ls .claude/tools/` and run `<path> --help` to learn each tool's purpose and usage. Invoke them via the Bash tool. Project MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for informal mathematical reasoning. Useful when you need a second opinion or a paper-style proof sketch the Lean side can then formalize.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (see "Subagents" below). One wrapper handles every subagent.
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`. Each starts with YAML frontmatter (`name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`) followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude session reads.
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents yourself.** The plan and review prompts have an auto-generated **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation that lists every enabled descriptor with its description, write-domain hint, and any `[MANDATORY]` flags. When you decide to invoke a specific subagent, read `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` for its full prompt and directive shape.
31	
32	**Invoke any subagent with the generic wrapper** (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, and writes the subagent's report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. It shells out to `archon subagent <name>`, so executions stream through the Archon JSONL log like a phase agent. The dispatch semaphore bounds total concurrent subagent processes by `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` automatically so deeper-nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Always dispatch synchronously** (not via background `Bash` with `run_in_background: true`). The wrapper returns when the subagent has finished and the report is on disk; only then write your session summary. Background dispatch leaves the parent's session permanently summarized as "running in background", which is wrong as soon as the subagent completes.
45	
46	**Mandatory subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` MUST be dispatched at least once during the plan phase (similarly for `[review]`). The catalog tags them `[MANDATORY]`. A post-phase audit warns when a mandatory dispatch is missing.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All state files are in `.archon/`:
51	
52	| File | Plan Agent | Prover Agent | Subagents | Review Agent | User |
53	|------|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|------|
54	| `.archon/PROGRESS.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
55	| `.archon/STRATEGY.md` | **read + write** | do not read | do not read | do not read | read |
56	| `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` | read (then clear) | do not read | do not read | do not read | write |
57	| `.archon/task_pending.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
58	| `.archon/task_done.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
59	| `.archon/task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect results) | write (own file only) | write (`<name>-<slug>.md`) | read only | read |
60	| `.archon/proof-journal/` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
61	| `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log moved to iter/iter-NNN/review.md) | read |
62	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | do not access | do not access | read (last K iters as context) | read |
63	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (last K iters as context) | do not access | do not access | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write (per-attempt detail) | do not access | do not access | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | **read** | **read** | **read** (write-capable subagents may rename file path only) | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | do not edit | write (own file only, frozen protected signatures) | per descriptor: write-capable subagents (e.g. refactor) may edit per their declared write-domain (protected decls may be moved but not renamed/re-signed); read-only subagents do not edit | do not edit | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints, structure) | do not edit | do not edit | **write** (markers only: `\leanok`, `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections) | read |
68	| `Challenges/<Name>.lean` | do not edit | fill sorries (when assigned) | write-capable when the descriptor's write_domain covers it | do not edit | read |
69	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | do not access | write-capable when the descriptor's write_domain covers it | do not access | read |
70	
71	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow, run by hand by the mathematician.** The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. The plan agent invokes the refactor subagent (when a `refactor` descriptor is installed) via the generic `archon-subagent.py --name refactor` wrapper with a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/`. This keeps each loop-driven refactor independently logged and avoids any chance of stale-directive reuse. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`, etc.) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise and prune them out as you rewrite.
72	
73	## Protected Declarations
74	
75	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Every agent must consult it before editing a `.lean` file. Rules:
76	
77	- **Plan / prover / review agents**: read-only on protected signatures. You may fill proof bodies, but not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
78	- **Refactor agent**: may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (keeping name + signature verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. Refactor agents may never rename, re-type, delete, or re-sign a protected declaration.
79	- The `archon-protected.yaml` file can only be edited by the user, no declaration can be added or removed by any agent. The only allowed modification is updating the file path of an existing protected declaration when its location was changed.
80	
81	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
82	
83	The blueprint uses two active markers.
84	
85	- `\leanok` — inside a statement block when the declaration is formalized with at least a `sorry`; inside a proof block when the proof is fully closed with no `sorry`.
86	- `\mathlibok` — inside a statement block when the declaration already exists in Mathlib and the Archon side is a re-export/alias; no Archon proof obligation remains.
87	- No marker — the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
88	
89	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between the prover and review phases each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves — let the script do it.
90	
91	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations remain the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
92	
93	## User Interaction
94	
95	Users provide hints in two places:
96	
97	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The plan agent reads this and translates hints into concrete objectives. Provers never read this file.
98	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
99	
100	## Agent Roles
101	
102	### Plan Agent
103	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for your full instructions
104	- Read `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate hints, then clear them after acting
105	- Read `.archon/task_results/` — collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md`
106	- Optionally invoke **subagents** (discoverable via `ls .archon/subagents/`) by running `.claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name <name> ...` via Bash, **before** writing prover objectives. See `.archon/prompts/plan.md` § "Subagent delegation" for the canonical ordering and full invocation pattern.
107	- Archive any subagent report you receive to `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` so the dashboard can render it.
108	- Write `.archon/PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round
109	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (except for marker updates) and `\lean{...}` hints for the provers
110	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself
111	
112	### Prover Agent
113	- Read `.archon/PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it)
114	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/`:
115	  - autoformalize → `.archon/prompts/prover-autoformalize.md`
116	  - prover → `.archon/prompts/prover-prover.md`
117	  - polish → `.archon/prompts/prover-polish.md`
118	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md`
119	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — **never edit another agent's file**
120	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your `.lean` file for file-specific hints
121	- **Do NOT edit blueprint chapters.** Marker updates are the review agent's responsibility. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker.
122	
123	### Subagents
124	
125	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime — there is no fixed roster baked into this file. Each lives at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter + prompt body). To see what's available **this iteration**, run `ls .archon/subagents/` and read each descriptor's frontmatter for purpose, write-domain hint, and directive shape.
126	
127	Subagents are **not** part of the autonomous loop's standing phases. Phase agents (plan, review) invoke them on demand via the generic wrapper:
128	
129	```
130	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name <name> --slug <slug> --directive-file <path> --write-domain '<glob>'
131	```
132	
133	Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, or other phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. The dispatching agent reads the report when the wrapper returns and may then update `STRATEGY.md` / `PROGRESS.md` based on the findings.
134	
135	Some descriptors set `can_spawn: true`, meaning that subagent may itself dispatch children; the per-iter dispatch semaphore caps total concurrent processes by `loop.max_parallel` so deep trees stay bounded.
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for your full instructions
139	- Read `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data
140	- Write session journal to `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (summary.md, milestones.jsonl, recommendations.md)
141	- Update `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` with overall progress
142	- Maintain the **semantic** blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE: ...` annotations, stale `\notready` removal — in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch `\leanok`; it's handled by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase that ran before you.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify PROGRESS.md
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	Two non-agent steps run automatically each iteration to reduce the burden on the agents above:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init**: at iter start, `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` is created so the plan + review agents have a stable destination for their per-iter narrative (`plan.md`, `review.md`, optional `objectives.md`). Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded across iters because per-iter content lives in the sidecars.
150	- **`sync_leanok`**: runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status. Replaces what used to be a multi-page review-agent task.
151	
152	`sync_leanok` writes an inner-git commit (`archon[NNN/marker-sync]`) so its output is auditable and revertable.

1	# Review Agent — Post-Session Proof Journal + Analysis
2	
3	You are the review agent. Your job is to: (1) analyze the most recent prover session with fine-grained detail, (2) produce a structured proof journal, (3) update project status, (4) maintain the blueprint markers that require *semantic* judgement (`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations), and (5) write recommendations for the next plan iteration.
4	
5	`\leanok` placement is handled deterministically by Archon's `sync_leanok` phase, which runs immediately before you. **Do not add or remove `\leanok` yourself.** If a `\leanok` you expect is missing, the underlying Lean source still has a sorry or doesn't compile — investigate, don't paper over.
6	
7	**Do NOT modify any .lean files. Do NOT write proofs.** You may run `lean_diagnostic_messages` / `lake env lean <file>` to verify compilation and sorry counts as part of your analysis. Otherwise you only read logs, analyze, and write journal/status/blueprint-marker files.
8	
9	## Iteration number — canonical
10	
11	Your invocation prompt contains `Archon iteration: NNN` and `Session number: M`. As of 2026 these are the **same number** — `session_M/` is always the review of `iter-NNN` (i.e. M == NNN). Use the iteration form (`iter-NNN`) when referencing the iteration in prose (`summary.md` metadata, recommendations titles like "Recommendations for the next plan-agent iteration (iter-{NNN+1:03d})") and the bare integer (`session_M`) when referring to the review-output directory. If older `summary.md` / `recommendations.md` files use the legacy independent session counter, treat them as drift from earlier agents — your new file uses the iteration number.
12	
13	## Step 1: Identify Context
14	
15	1. Check `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/` — count existing session folders to determine the current session number.
16	2. Run `find ${PROJECT_PATH} -name '*.lean' -not -path '*/.lake/*' -not -path '*/lake-packages/*' | xargs grep -c 'sorry' 2>/dev/null | grep -v ':0$' | awk -F: '{s+=$2} END {print s}'` for the current sorry count.
17	3. Run `git diff HEAD~1 --stat` to see what changed.
18	
19	## Step 2: Read Pre-processed Attempt Data (MANDATORY)
20	
21	**READ `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` COMPLETELY.** If this file does not exist or is empty, report it and proceed with what you can gather from task_results.
22	
23	The file contains:
24	
25	- Line 1: Summary stats (`type: "summary"` — total edits, goal checks, errors, files edited)
26	- Remaining lines: One event per tool call — edits, goal states, errors, lemma searches, builds
27	
28	**For each `code_change` event**: Record the actual code that was tried (old_text → new_text).
29	**For each `goal_state` event**: Record the Lean goal at that point.
30	**For each `diagnostics` event**: Record the Lean errors.
31	**For each `build` event**: Record whether it succeeded or failed.
32	
33	This is your PRIMARY data source. Task result files are supplementary.
34	
35	## Step 3: Read Recent History
36	
37	If previous session folders exist in `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/`, read `summary.md` from the **last 2 sessions**. Also read `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` if it exists.
38	
39	## Step 4: Write Proof Journal
40	
41	Create the session folder and write two files:
42	
43	```bash
44	mkdir -p .archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>
45	```
46	
47	### File A: `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/summary.md`
48	
49	Must include:
50	- Session metadata (number, sorry count before/after, targets attempted)
51	- For EACH target attempted:
52	  - **Every significant attempt** with: tactic/code tried, Lean error received, goal state at that point
53	  - What was learned from each failed attempt
54	  - For solved targets: the final proof structure with key lemmas
55	- Key findings / proof patterns discovered
56	- Recommendations for next session
57	
58	### File B: `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/milestones.jsonl`
59	
60	Each line MUST follow this JSON format — one entry per target theorem:
61	
62	```json
63	{
64	  "timestamp": "ISO-8601",
65	  "status": "solved|partial|blocked|not_started",
66	  "target": {
67	    "file": "path/to/File.lean",
68	    "theorem": "theorem_name"
69	  },
70	  "session": {
71	    "id": "session_N",
72	    "model": "model-name"
73	  },
74	  "findings": {
75	    "blocker": "description if blocked",
76	    "key_lemmas_used": ["lemma1", "lemma2"]
77	  },
78	  "attempts": [
79	    {
80	      "attempt": 1,
81	      "strategy": "what was tried",
82	      "code_tried": "actual Lean code or tactic",
83	      "line_number": "line number in the initial file where this code was attempted",
84	      "lean_error": "actual error message if failed",
85	      "goal_before": "the goal state before this attempt",
86	      "goal_after": "the goal state after this attempt",
87	      "result": "success|failed|partial",
88	      "insight": "what was learned from this attempt"
89	    }
90	  ],
91	  "next_steps": "..."
92	}
93	```
94	
95	**CRITICAL**: The `attempts` array must reflect ACTUAL attempts from the pre-processed data:
96	- If `attempts_raw.jsonl` shows 5 edits to a file, there should be multiple attempts recorded
97	- Each attempt must include `code_tried` (from edit events) and `lean_error` (from diagnostic events)
98	- Do NOT summarize multiple attempts as "tried various approaches" — list each one
99	
100	### File C: `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/recommendations.md`
101	
102	Write concrete recommendations for the next plan agent iteration:
103	- Which targets are closest to completion and should be prioritized
104	- Which approaches showed promise but need more work
105	- Which targets are blocked and why (the plan agent should NOT assign these)
106	- Any reusable proof patterns discovered
107	
108	If your analysis shows the prover has hit the exact same blocker for several consecutive iterations on the same target, you should explicitly instruct the Plan Agent to avoid retrying the same approach without putting more effort into understanding the underlying issue.
109	
110	## Step 5: Update PROJECT_STATUS.md (Knowledge Base only)
111	
112	The per-session narrative (Overall Progress: total sorry, branches closed, solved/partial/blocked/untouched, this session's analysis) goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` — see the next subsection. PROJECT_STATUS.md carries only the cumulative Knowledge Base:
113	
114	```markdown
115	# Project Status
116	
117	## Knowledge Base
118	### Proof Patterns (reusable across targets)
119	- <pattern name>: <description + key lemmas>
120	
121	### Known Blockers (do not retry)
122	- <target>: <reason>
123	
124	## Last Updated
125	<ISO timestamp>
126	```
127	
128	If an existing PROJECT_STATUS.md still carries an "Overall Progress" section from a prior project that grew it, leave the old content where it is (do not delete history) but stop appending to it. New session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`.
129	
130	### Per-iteration sidecars (split write)
131	
132	Your invocation prompt contains a `## Per-iteration sidecars` block that names `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` as the destination for THIS session's narrative. Split your writes accordingly:
133	
134	- **The per-session "Overall Progress" narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`**, NOT to PROJECT_STATUS.md's "Overall Progress" section. PROJECT_STATUS.md no longer carries a session log.
135	- **You DO keep updating PROJECT_STATUS.md's "Knowledge Base" section.** Cumulative non-obvious facts (errors not to reproduce, reusable proof patterns, Mathlib idioms that worked) still belong in the Knowledge Base. The Knowledge Base is the only growing-but-curated part of PROJECT_STATUS.md.
136	- **Format for `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`**: same fields as the Overall Progress block above (Total sorry, branches closed, solved/partial/blocked/untouched, plus any narrative analysis). Born-bounded — this file contains THIS session only, never a multi-session log.
137	- Older `iter/iter-MMM/review.md` files are on disk for you to read on demand, but the recent window is already injected in your prompt.
138	
139	## Step 6: Blueprint Markers
140	
141	`\leanok` placement is now handled deterministically by Archon's `sync_leanok` phase, which runs between the prover and you. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration in the Lean source, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. **Do not touch `\leanok` markers yourself.** If you see one missing where you expect it, the underlying file probably doesn't compile or still has a sorry — do not paper over that with a manual edit.
142	
143	Your remaining marker responsibilities are the ones that require semantic judgement:
144	
145	- **`\mathlibok`** (statement-block only) — declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias / direct reference). The deterministic script never adds or removes this; you decide based on the prover's task result.
146	- **`\lean{...}` macro maintenance** — if a prover renamed a declaration or chose a different name from the plan agent's hint, their task result will mention it. Update the `\lean{...}` line in the chapter to the correct Lean name. If a declaration was moved to a different file by a refactor, update the chapter's location references as needed.
147	- **`% NOTE: <reason>` annotations** — when a block is unformalized because the informal statement did not translate cleanly, add a `% NOTE: ...` comment explaining the obstacle so the plan agent sees it.
148	- **Stripping `\notready`** — if a `\notready` marker still sits on a block that the prover has now landed, remove it. The deterministic script does not manage `\notready`.
149	
150	### `\mathlibok` rules (your domain)
151	
152	Add `\mathlibok` inside the statement block when:
153	- The Lean side references a Mathlib name directly (`def foo := Mathlib.bar`, `theorem foo := Mathlib.bar`, or a simple `export Mathlib.Foo (bar)`), AND
154	- The Archon-side declaration itself contains no `sorry` and introduces no new proof obligation.
155	
156	If you add `\mathlibok`, no `\leanok` is needed on the proof block — the deterministic script will leave the proof block alone if there's no Lean proof body to verify.
157	
158	### Record what you changed
159	
160	In `session_<N>/summary.md`, include a "Blueprint markers updated" section listing **only the changes you personally made** (the deterministic script's `\leanok` adds/removes are committed separately as `archon[NNN/marker-sync]` and don't need to appear here):
161	
162	```markdown
163	## Blueprint markers updated (manual)
164	- `Algebra_WLocal.tex`, `lem:finite_closed`: added `\mathlibok` (backed by `Set.Finite.isClosed`)
165	- `Core.tex`, `thm:stacks_0A31`: added `% NOTE: prover reported translation gap, see task_results/Core.md`
166	- `Core.tex`, `thm:old_name`: stripped stale `\notready`
167	- `Core.tex`, `thm:foo`: corrected `\lean{Old.foo}` → `\lean{New.foo}` after refactor rename
168	```
169	
170	### File D: `.archon/TO_USER.md`
171	
172	While the agents should be autonomous, you might want to inform the user of any critical issues that require their attention. The content of `TO_USER.md` will be surfaced in the UI as an alert banner, however the user might not see it immediately or never see it at all (its content will be refreshed every iteration before the review agent is called). It might include for instance issues with the environment, critical missing dependencies, required user actions, etc. 
173	
174	**Always check the planner's iter sidecar for escalations.** Read `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` (this iter's plan-agent narrative). If the planner intentionally skipped provers — signaled by the `(no prover dispatch this iter ...)` marker in `PROGRESS.md`'s `## Current Objectives` section, by a "user escalation" / "hard gate fired" notice in the sidecar, or by the planner's `planValidate.status: ok_intentional_skip` meta — you MUST write a concise alert banner to `TO_USER.md` naming:
175	- What the impasse is (one sentence).
176	- What options the planner has surfaced (one bullet per option, if applicable).
177	- Where to find full context (the sidecar path).
178	
179	The planner does NOT write to `USER_HINTS.md` for escalations — `USER_HINTS.md` is user-to-planner only. The escalation banner is your responsibility; you are the user-facing channel.
180	
181	**Rules:**
182	- Be extremely concise (1-2 sentences per item, listed in markdown format).
183	- If nothing relevant for the user is detected, leave the file completely empty.
184	
185	## Step 7: Dispatch review subagents
186	
187	You may dispatch read-only review subagents BEFORE writing your consolidated summary. They are audits whose findings you incorporate into `summary.md` / `recommendations.md`. Each runs as its own fresh-context Claude process; spawning two or more in one assistant message runs them in parallel, subject to the global `max_parallel` cap.
188	
189	### Where the catalog comes from
190	
191	Your invocation prompt contains an auto-generated **Available subagents** section listing every enabled descriptor. Reviewers are typically marked `[read-only]`. A descriptor tagged `[MANDATORY]` for this phase MUST be dispatched before the phase completes. Do not `ls .archon/subagents/` — the catalog you were handed is authoritative.
192	
193	When you decide to invoke a specific reviewer, read its full prompt and directive shape from `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
194	
195	### How to dispatch
196	
197	Pattern (Bash tool, parallel-able, run **in foreground** so the report lands before you write your summary):
198	
199	```
200	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
201	  --name <subagent-name> \
202	  --slug <kebab-case-slug> \
203	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md \
204	  --write-domain 'task_results/**'
205	```
206	
207	Each directive must be **fully self-contained** — the reviewer reads only what the directive points it at. The directive shape for each reviewer is documented in its descriptor under `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
208	
209	### When NOT to dispatch
210	
211	- If this session was a pure proof-filling round with no new definitions or refactors, skip reviewers. They add latency and cost for no value.
212	- If a previous session in the last 3 iters already ran the same audit on the same scope, don't repeat unless that scope changed.
213	- The plan agent may also have dispatched reviewers proactively — check `task_results/` for matching reports before doubling up.
214	
215	### Incorporating findings
216	
217	After the reviewers return, read every report. Land each finding in your `summary.md` and `recommendations.md`:
218	
219	- **CRITICAL / HIGH** findings → bullet at the top of `recommendations.md` with the suggested action.
220	- **MEDIUM** findings → bullet in `recommendations.md`'s body.
221	- **LOW** findings → one-liner in `summary.md`'s notes section.
222	
223	Do NOT repeat the full report content in your summary — link to the report file. The plan agent reads your summary, not the raw reports.
224	
225	## Step 8: Self-Validation
226	
227	After writing all files, validate your output by checking:
228	- [ ] milestones.jsonl has valid JSON on every line
229	- [ ] Each milestone has `target.file`, `target.theorem`, `status`
230	- [ ] Each non-blocked milestone has at least 1 attempt with `code_tried` or `strategy`
231	- [ ] Number of attempts per milestone is proportional to edits in `attempts_raw.jsonl`
232	- [ ] summary.md includes specific code/errors, not just high-level summaries
233	- [ ] recommendations.md includes actionable next steps
234	- [ ] You did NOT add or remove any `\leanok` marker yourself (those are handled by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase that ran before you)
235	- [ ] For every Mathlib-backed declaration the prover reported, the blueprint chapter has `\mathlibok` (your domain)
236	- [ ] Any `\lean{...}` macro rename flagged in a `task_results/<file>.md` has been applied
237	- [ ] No `\notready` marker remains on a block whose Lean declaration now exists
238	
239	## Permissions
240	
241	You may write to:
242	- `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/` (summary.md, milestones.jsonl, recommendations.md)
243	- `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md`
244	- `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` — markers (`\leanok`, `\mathlibok`), `\lean{...}` macro corrections, stale-marker cleanup, and `% NOTE:` comments. Do NOT rewrite the informal prose — that is the plan agent's surface.
245	
246	You must NOT write to:
247	- Any `.lean` files
248	- `.archon/PROGRESS.md` (plan agent's responsibility)
249	- `.archon/task_pending.md` or `.archon/task_done.md` (plan agent's responsibility)
250	- Blueprint informal content (theorem/proof prose, chapter structure) — only the markers and `% NOTE:` comments are yours.
251

1	# Iter-117 (Archon canonical) plan-agent run
2	
3	## Headline outcome
4	
5	**Aggressive TRIM landed** in response to a substantive user directive
6	in `USER_HINTS.md` (clear, autonomous, strict correctness, detailed
7	blueprints, clean strategy, no deferrals). The plan agent rewrote
8	`STRATEGY.md` from scratch, dispatched 4 critics + 1 refactor + 3
9	blueprint-writers, and verified the post-trim project state.

12	deletion). The two survivors are:

15	  form, prover-closeable iter-118 via verified Mathlib bridges).
16	- `Jacobian.lean:179` `nonempty_jacobianWitness` (the single

29	> yourself, you should remove all wrong mathematical statements and
30	> plan how to fill the gap it leaves. No wrong definition/proofs/
31	> signatures are accepted it should always be correct and never be
32	> temporarily wrong. Moreover the blueprints should be detailed enough
33	> to ensure that the provers have enough material. Moreover, the
34	> strategy file is too messy, it should be more clearly organize
35	> without making it an endless prose, it should focus on the strategy
36	> not enumerated all of the achievements in the previous steps,
37	> nothing should be deferred.
38

42	  architectural commitment (defer named Mathlib gaps as the end-state)
43	  is exactly what the user overruled. Recommended **aggressive TRIM**:
44	  delete every orphan-to-protected-chain sorry by deleting the
45	  containing declaration. For the protected `nonempty_jacobianWitness`
46	  the report listed three options (drop from `archon-protected.yaml`,
47	  multi-month BUILD, waive no-deferral for this one sorry).

56	  open Čech "DEFERRED (budget)" annotation). Default response: either
57	  decompose each into actionable subclaims or contract scope.

59	- **progress-critic-iter117 — Routes 1+4 STUCK; Routes 2+3 UNCLEAR.**
60	  Route 1 = L191 unique-gluing, 4-of-5 iters with the affine-basis-
61	  bridge blocker phrase; primary corrective = refactor restructuring
62	  surfacing the gap as a named lemma OR delete. Route 4 = L1120
63	  `cechCofaceMap_pi_smul`, 7+ PARTIAL iters + 9 parked iters; primary
64	  corrective = architectural refactor (Q2 Path B/A) before any further
65	  prover lane. Route 2 = L1846 budget-deferred, parked-not-stuck.
66	  Route 3 = L931 fresh, well-scaffolded, highest expected information
67	  yield for a first prover lane.

70	  sorry sites is on a **true** mathematical statement. No false
71	  signatures, no stand-in definitions, no excuse-comments, no axioms.
72	  The project is mathematically honest; the user's "remove all wrong
73	  mathematical statements" requirement is trivially satisfied because
74	  there are none.

5	-/
6	import Mathlib.RingTheory.Kaehler.Basic
7	import Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme
8	import Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.FinitePresentation
9	import Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Smooth
10	import Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Differentials.Presheaf
11	import Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Presheaf
12	
13	/-! # Relative Kähler differentials for schemes (presheaf form)
14	
15	This file constructs the **presheaf** of relative Kähler differentials
16	`Ω_{X/S}` of a morphism of schemes `f : X → S` and states the
17	characterisation of smoothness in terms of pointwise local freeness of
18	`Ω` on affine opens.
19	
20	The construction builds on Mathlib's ring-theoretic `KaehlerDifferential`
21	and the presheaf-of-modules differential construction
22	`PresheafOfModules.DifferentialsConstruction.relativeDifferentials'`.
23	
24	## References
25	
26	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Differentials.tex`.
27	-/
28	
29	set_option autoImplicit false
30	
31	universe u
32	
33	open CategoryTheory Limits TopologicalSpace AlgebraicGeometry
34	
35	namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme
36	
37	variable {X S : Scheme.{u}}
38	
39	/-- The relative cotangent **presheaf** of a morphism of schemes `f : X ⟶ S`.
40	
41	On each open `U ⊆ X`, the sections are the Kähler differential module of
42	the ring map `O_S(f(U)) → O_X(U)` induced by `f`. More precisely, we use
43	the inverse-image presheaf `f⁻¹ O_S` on `X` and the canonical map to `O_X`;
44	the Kähler differential construction then gives a presheaf of `O_X`-modules.
45	
46	Mathlib leverage: `TopCat.Presheaf.pullback` for `f⁻¹`,
47	`PresheafOfModules.DifferentialsConstruction.relativeDifferentials'`
48	for the presheaf of Kähler differentials. -/
49	noncomputable def relativeDifferentialsPresheaf (f : X ⟶ S) : X.PresheafOfModules :=
50	  let φ' := ((TopCat.Presheaf.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction CommRingCat f.base).homEquiv _ _).symm
51	    f.c
52	  PresheafOfModules.DifferentialsConstruction.relativeDifferentials' φ'
53	
54	/-- Localisation-compatibility identity: on each open `V ⊆ X`, the sections
55	of `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf f` over `V` are *definitionally* the
56	Kähler differential module of the ring map
57	`(f⁻¹ O_S)(V) → O_X(V)`. -/
58	theorem relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_obj_kaehler (f : X ⟶ S)
59	    (V : (TopologicalSpace.Opens X.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ) :
60	    ((relativeDifferentialsPresheaf f).presheaf.obj V : Type _) =
61	      CommRingCat.KaehlerDifferential
62	        (((TopCat.Presheaf.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction CommRingCat
63	          f.base).homEquiv _ _).symm f.c |>.app V) :=
64	  rfl
65	
66	/-- Smoothness of a finite-presentation morphism is equivalent to the
67	relative differential presheaf being locally free of the given rank on
68	every affine open.
69	
70	The forward direction is the Jacobian criterion via
71	`Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.basis_kaehlerDifferential`
72	on affine charts; the converse follows from
73	`Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.iff_exists_basis_kaehlerDifferential`. -/
74	theorem smooth_iff_locally_free_omega (f : X ⟶ S)
75	    (hfp : AlgebraicGeometry.LocallyOfFinitePresentation f) (n : ℕ) :
76	    AlgebraicGeometry.IsSmoothOfRelativeDimension n f ↔
77	      ∀ (x : X), ∃ (U : X.Opens), x ∈ U.1 ∧ IsAffineOpen U ∧
78	        let R := X.ringCatSheaf.presheaf.obj (.op U)
79	        let M := (relativeDifferentialsPresheaf f).presheaf.obj (.op U)
80	        Module.Free (↑R) (↑M) ∧ Module.rank (↑R) (↑M) = n := by
81	  sorry
82	
83	end AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme
84

1	/-

4	Authors: Christian Merten

6	import AlgebraicJacobian.Genus
7	
8	/-! # The Jacobian of a smooth proper curve
9	
10	The Jacobian of a smooth, proper, geometrically irreducible curve over a field, equipped with
11	its structure as an abelian variety.
12	
13	## Status (Phase C scaffolding)
14	
15	The Jacobian is defined uniformly as the underlying scheme of an Albanese witness
16	(`JacobianWitness`) for the curve `C`. Existence of such a witness is the single
17	remaining mathematical sorry of the Phase-C scaffolding (`nonempty_jacobianWitness`);
18	the four protected instances on `Jacobian C` all project from the witness directly.
19	
20	The file contains:
21	- `IsAlbanese`: the Albanese universal property for a pointed curve.
22	- `IsAlbanese.unique`: uniqueness of the Albanese object up to canonical isomorphism.
23	- `JacobianWitness`: a bundled candidate Albanese object together with the universal
24	  property uniformly over the choice of $k$-rational marked point.
25	- `nonempty_jacobianWitness`: the (deferred) existence of such a witness — the single
26	  Phase-C sorry, absorbing both higher-genus Albanese existence and genus-`0` rigidity.
27	- `Jacobian` and its four protected instances, each obtained by projection from the
28	  Albanese witness.
29	
30	### Forbidden shortcut (sanity check)
31	
32	Defining `Jacobian C := 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of k)))` *unconditionally* (the terminal object,
33	i.e. `Spec k`) compiles and discharges three of the four instances for free, but the
34	fourth instance `SmoothOfRelativeDimension (genus C) (𝟙_ _).hom` would force `genus C = 0`,
35	which is mathematically wrong for any curve of genus `≥ 1`. The terminal-object definition
36	is therefore forbidden when `genus C > 0`. The witness-based definition avoids this
37	issue: in genus `0` the witness's underlying scheme `J` is `Spec k`, and in genus `> 0`
38	the witness's `J` is the honest Albanese object.
39	-/
40	
41	set_option autoImplicit false
42	
43	universe u
44	
45	open CategoryTheory Limits MonoidalCategory CartesianMonoidalCategory MonObj
46	
47	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
48	
49	variable {k : Type u} [Field k] (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
50	  [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
51	  [IsProper C.hom]
52	  [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
53	
54	/-- The Albanese universal property for a pointed smooth proper curve `(C, P)`.
55	An object `J` (with abelian-variety structure) is the Albanese of `(C, P)` if it
56	receives a universal pointed morphism `C ⟶ J` sending `P` to the identity. -/
57	def IsAlbanese (C : Over (Spec (.of k))) (P : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of k))) ⟶ C)
58	    (J : Over (Spec (.of k)))
59	    [GrpObj J] [IsProper J.hom] [Smooth J.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible J.hom] : Prop :=
60	  ∃ α : C ⟶ J, P ≫ α = η[J] ∧
61	    ∀ {A : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom]
62	      [Smooth A.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom] (f : C ⟶ A) (_ : P ≫ f = η[A]),
63	      ∃! (g : J ⟶ A), f = α ≫ g
64	
65	namespace IsAlbanese
66	
67	noncomputable def ofCurve {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
68	    {P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C} {J : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj J] [IsProper J.hom] [Smooth J.hom]
69	    [GeometricallyIrreducible J.hom] (h : IsAlbanese C P J) : C ⟶ J :=
70	  Classical.choose h
71	
72	lemma comp_ofCurve {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
73	    {P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C} {J : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj J] [IsProper J.hom] [Smooth J.hom]
74	    [GeometricallyIrreducible J.hom] (h : IsAlbanese C P J) :
75	    P ≫ h.ofCurve = η[J] :=
76	  (Classical.choose_spec h).1
77	
78	lemma exists_unique_ofCurve_comp {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
79	    {P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C} {J : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj J] [IsProper J.hom] [Smooth J.hom]
80	    [GeometricallyIrreducible J.hom] (h : IsAlbanese C P J)
81	    {A : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom] [Smooth A.hom]
82	    [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom] (f : C ⟶ A) (hf : P ≫ f = η[A]) :
83	    ∃! (g : J ⟶ A), f = h.ofCurve ≫ g :=
84	  (Classical.choose_spec h).2 f hf
85	
86	/-- The Albanese object is unique up to a unique isomorphism compatible with the
87	universal morphisms. -/
88	theorem unique {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))} {P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C}
89	    {J₁ J₂ : Over (Spec (.of k))}
90	    [GrpObj J₁] [IsProper J₁.hom] [Smooth J₁.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible J₁.hom]
91	    [GrpObj J₂] [IsProper J₂.hom] [Smooth J₂.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible J₂.hom]
92	    (h₁ : IsAlbanese C P J₁) (h₂ : IsAlbanese C P J₂) :
93	    ∃! (e : J₁ ⟶ J₂), h₂.ofCurve = h₁.ofCurve ≫ e := by
94	  have ⟨g, hg_eq, hg_unique⟩ := h₁.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp h₂.ofCurve h₂.comp_ofCurve
95	  have ⟨h, hh_eq, hh_unique⟩ := h₂.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp h₁.ofCurve h₁.comp_ofCurve
96	  have ⟨g₁, hg₁_eq, hg₁_unique⟩ := h₁.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp h₁.ofCurve h₁.comp_ofCurve
97	  have g₁_eq_id : g₁ = 𝟙 J₁ := by
98	    refine (hg₁_unique (𝟙 J₁) ?_).symm
99	    simp
100	  have ggh_eq_g₁ : g ≫ h = g₁ := by
101	    refine hg₁_unique (g ≫ h) ?_
102	    change h₁.ofCurve = h₁.ofCurve ≫ (g ≫ h)
103	    rw [← Category.assoc, ← hg_eq, ← hh_eq]
104	  have hgh : g ≫ h = 𝟙 J₁ := by rw [ggh_eq_g₁, g₁_eq_id]
105	  have ⟨k₂, hk₂_eq, hk₂_unique⟩ := h₂.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp h₂.ofCurve h₂.comp_ofCurve
106	  have k₂_eq_id : k₂ = 𝟙 J₂ := by
107	    refine (hk₂_unique (𝟙 J₂) ?_).symm
108	    simp
109	  have hhg_eq_k₂ : h ≫ g = k₂ := by
110	    refine hk₂_unique (h ≫ g) ?_
111	    change h₂.ofCurve = h₂.ofCurve ≫ (h ≫ g)
112	    rw [← Category.assoc, ← hh_eq, ← hg_eq]
113	  have hhg : h ≫ g = 𝟙 J₂ := by rw [hhg_eq_k₂, k₂_eq_id]
114	  exact ⟨g, hg_eq, fun g' hg' => hg_unique g' hg'⟩
115	
116	end IsAlbanese
117	
118	/-- The identity morphism on `Spec k` is geometrically irreducible.
119	This is a small helper needed for the genus-0 case of `Jacobian`. -/
120	lemma geometricallyIrreducible_id_Spec (k : Type u) [Field k] :
121	    GeometricallyIrreducible (𝟙 (Spec (.of k))) where
122	  geometrically_irreducibleSpace := by
123	    intro K _ y Z fst snd h
124	    haveI : IsIso snd := h.isIso_snd_of_isIso
125	    exact ObjectProperty.prop_of_iso (P := (IrreducibleSpace · : ObjectProperty Scheme))
126	      (asIso snd).symm inferInstance
127	
128	/-- A bundled Albanese witness for the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
129	`C`: a candidate group scheme `J` (with proper, smooth, geometrically irreducible
130	structure, smooth of relative dimension `genus C`), and a proof that `J` satisfies
131	the Albanese universal property of Definition `IsAlbanese` for `C` *for every choice
132	of $k$-rational marked point* `P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C`.
133	
134	Mathematically, the underlying scheme `J` (and its group/proper/smooth/irreducible
135	structure) is intrinsic to `C`; only the universal morphism `C ⟶ J` depends on the
136	choice of marked point `P`. Encoding `isAlbaneseFor` as a `∀ P, IsAlbanese …` field
137	keeps the bundle independent of any specific pointing, which is what
138	`AbelJacobi.Jacobian.ofCurve P` needs in order to project the Albanese morphism for
139	an *arbitrary* input `P`.
140	
141	The smoothness witness is recorded at the specific relative dimension `genus C`,
142	which is the form required by `Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus`. -/
143	structure JacobianWitness (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
144	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] where
145	  /-- The candidate Albanese object. -/
146	  J : Over (Spec (.of k))
147	  /-- The group-scheme structure on `J`. -/
148	  grpObj : GrpObj J
149	  /-- `J` is proper over `k`. -/
150	  proper : IsProper J.hom
151	  /-- `J` is smooth over `k`. -/
152	  smooth : Smooth J.hom
153	  /-- `J` is geometrically irreducible over `k`. -/
154	  geomIrred : GeometricallyIrreducible J.hom
155	  /-- `J` is smooth of relative dimension `genus C` (so dim `J = genus C`). -/
156	  smoothGenus : SmoothOfRelativeDimension (genus C) J.hom
157	  /-- The Albanese universal-property data: for every choice of $k$-rational marked
158	  point `P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C`, the scheme `J` together with its group/proper/smooth/irreducible
159	  structure satisfies the Albanese universal property for the pointed curve `(C, P)`. -/
160	  isAlbaneseFor : ∀ (P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C), @IsAlbanese k _ C P J grpObj proper smooth geomIrred
161	
162	/-- Existence of an Albanese witness for every smooth proper geometrically irreducible
163	curve. This is the single remaining mathematical sorry of the Phase-C Jacobian
164	scaffolding: it packages all five protected sorries (`Jacobian`, `instGrpObj`,
165	`smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus`, `instIsProper`, `instGeometricallyIrreducible`)
166	into a single existence hypothesis, *and* records that the Albanese property of the
167	underlying scheme `J` is uniform over every choice of $k$-rational marked point
168	`P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C` (via the `isAlbaneseFor` field of `JacobianWitness`).
169	
170	Mathematically this corresponds to the construction of the Albanese variety of `C`,
171	classically via symmetric powers $\mathrm{Sym}^g(C)$ and the Abel--Jacobi map
172	(Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch), or equivalently as the identity component of the
173	Picard scheme. Both routes require infrastructure not yet available in Mathlib
174	(quotients of schemes by finite group actions; FGA representability), so the
175	existence is assumed and the proof is deferred to a future iteration. -/
176	theorem nonempty_jacobianWitness (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
177	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] :
178	    Nonempty (JacobianWitness C) :=
179	  sorry
180	
181	/-- A choice of Albanese witness for `C`, extracted via `Classical.choice`.
182	Used to define `Jacobian C` and to discharge each of the four protected instances
183	on it. -/
184	noncomputable def jacobianWitness (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
185	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] :
186	    JacobianWitness C :=
187	  Classical.choice (nonempty_jacobianWitness C)
188	
189	-- data
190	/-- The Jacobian of a smooth, proper curve over a field `k`.
191	
192	The Jacobian is the underlying scheme of an Albanese witness for `C` (see
193	`JacobianWitness` and `nonempty_jacobianWitness`); the existence of such a
194	witness is the single remaining mathematical sorry of the Phase-C scaffolding.
195	The genus-`0` specialisation is implicit in the witness — a smooth proper
196	geometrically irreducible group scheme over `k` of relative dimension `0` is
197	`Spec k` — and the genus-`0` rigidity content is absorbed into
198	`nonempty_jacobianWitness`. -/
199	noncomputable def Jacobian (C : Over (Spec (.of k))) [IsProper C.hom] [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
200	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] : Over (Spec (.of k)) :=
201	  (jacobianWitness C).J
202	
203	namespace Jacobian
204	
205	/-! ## The Jacobian of `C` is an abelian variety. -/
206	
207	-- data
208	/-- The group scheme structure on the Jacobian of the curve `C`. -/
209	noncomputable instance instGrpObj : GrpObj (Jacobian C) := (jacobianWitness C).grpObj
210	
211	/-- The Jacobian of `C` is smooth of relative dimension `g` over `k`, where `g` is the
212	genus of `C`. -/
213	instance smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus : SmoothOfRelativeDimension (genus C) (Jacobian C).hom :=
214	  (jacobianWitness C).smoothGenus
215	
216	/-- The Jacobian of `C` is proper over `k`. -/
217	instance instIsProper : IsProper (Jacobian C).hom := (jacobianWitness C).proper
218	
219	/-- The Jacobian of `C` is geometrically irreducible over `k`. -/
220	instance instGeometricallyIrreducible : GeometricallyIrreducible (Jacobian C).hom :=
221	  (jacobianWitness C).geomIrred
222	
223	end Jacobian
224	
225	end AlgebraicGeometry

26	# Lean Auditor
27	
28	You read **every `.lean` file in the project** and produce a per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, and bad Lean practices. You are **read-only** and your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
29	
30	You are **mandatory in the review phase**: the review agent dispatches you every iteration. Your output flags issues the prover and plan agents may have missed.
31	
32	## Stance: no strategic bias
33	
34	You audit the Lean **as Lean**. Your directive will NOT include the project strategy, the blueprint, or "what we are trying to prove" — and that is intentional. The point is to detect mathematically-wrong definitions and dead-end proofs *without* being swayed by what the project assumes should be true. A definition is wrong if it is wrong by reading the Lean alone (e.g. its body contradicts standard mathematical usage of the named concept); you do not need to know the strategy to spot that.
35	
36	This stance is also what makes you **critical of excuse-comments**. Lean-side comments like:
37	
38	- `-- We decided to use a temporary wrong definition; will fix it later.`
39	- `-- This proof is incomplete but works for now.`
40	- `-- TODO: replace before merging.`
41	- `-- Stand-in definition until we figure out the right one.`
42	
43	are RED FLAGS, not project workflow. Authors writing such comments are admitting the code is wrong; the comments cannot be allowed to silence the alarm. Flag every such comment with severity **major** at minimum — and **critical** when the named declaration is load-bearing.
44	
45	## Directive Format
46	
47	```markdown
48	# Lean Auditor Directive
49	
50	## Slug
51	<slug>
52	
53	## Scope (files)
54	<"all" by default. Optionally a list of file paths if the review agent wants to narrow scope; never expand beyond the project.>
55	
56	## Focus areas (optional)
57	<directories or namespaces to pay extra attention to. Bias toward thoroughness — do not skip the rest of the project just because focus is named.>
58	
59	## Known issues
60	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
61	```
62	
63	The directive **does not** include STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, references, or a description of what the project is trying to prove. That is by design.
64	
65	## What you do
66	
67	1. Read your directive.
68	2. **List every `.lean` file** under the project. Use `find` or `ls -R` — do not rely on import graphs (some files may be temporarily orphaned).
69	3. **For each file** (every one, no scope shortcuts):
70	   - Read the whole file.
71	   - For each declaration: is the signature reasonable as Lean? Is the body suspect (uses `axiom`, `Classical.choice _`, `sorry` with a body that looks like fake content, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim)?
72	   - For each comment: is it stale (referencing removed code or old strategies)? Is it an *excuse-comment* (see above)? Is it accurate about the code below it?
73	   - For each proof: is the tactic chain reasonable for the goal type, or does it look like it is going in the wrong direction (e.g. unfolding to manipulate syntax instead of using the available structure lemmas)?
74	   - Note any bad Lean practices: misuse of universe polymorphism, redundant typeclasses, deprecated tactics, anti-patterns like proving `False`-style lemmas to bypass infrastructure.
75	4. **Produce a per-file checklist** + a flagged-issues block grouped by severity.
76	
77	You may use:
78	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics. Useful to verify a declaration's actual type.
79	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to get sorry counts per file.
80	- Standard `Read` / `Grep` for code reading.

1	---

Reads one .lean file and its blueprint chapter; reports (a) whether the Lean file follows the blueprint — fake/placeholder statements, signature mismatches with \lean{...} hints, proof divergence, "temporary" defs — AND (b) whether the blueprint chapter is detailed enough to have guided a faithful formalization of this file.

35	
36	Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:
37	
38	### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?
39	
40	For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:
41	
42	- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
43	- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
44	- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
45	- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
46	- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
47	- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.
48

50	
51	The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:
52

54	- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.

Flag both sides.

1	\chapter{The relative cotangent presheaf}
2	\label{chap:differentials}
3	
4	This chapter develops the relative cotangent \emph{presheaf} $\Omega_{X/S}$ of a morphism of schemes $f \colon X \to S$, starting from the ring-theoretic K\"ahler differential module $\Omega_{B/A}$ already available in Mathlib. The output is a presheaf of $\struct{X}$-modules whose sections on each open $V \subseteq X$ are the K\"ahler differential module of the ring map $(f^{-1}\struct{S})(V) \to \struct{X}(V)$ induced by $f$.
5	
6	The downstream role of this chapter is to characterise smoothness of $f$ in terms of pointwise local freeness of $\Omega_{X/S}$ on an affine basis of $X$. This characterisation is what unlocks the dimension hypothesis on the Jacobian: the smoothness of the curve $C \to \Spec k$ in the Jacobian definition is the special case $n = 1$ of the criterion below, and the relative dimension of $\Jac(C)$ that appears in the smoothness statement is read off through the same criterion.
7	
8	\section{The relative cotangent presheaf}
9	
10	\begin{definition}
11	
12	\leanok
13	[Relative cotangent presheaf of a morphism]
14	  \label{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf}
15	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf}
16	  Let $f \colon X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes. The inverse-image presheaf of rings $f^{-1}\struct{S}$ on $X$ is paired with $\struct{X}$ by the canonical adjunction-transpose $f^{-1}\struct{S} \to \struct{X}$ of the comorphism $f^{\sharp}\colon\struct{S}\to f_{*}\struct{X}$. Applying the presheaf-of-modules K\"ahler differential construction $\mathrm{relativeDifferentials}'$ to this map of presheaves of commutative rings produces a presheaf of $\struct{X}$-modules on $X$, the \emph{relative cotangent presheaf} $\Omega_{X/S}$.
17	\end{definition}
18	
19	\begin{lemma}
20	
21	\leanok
22	[Sections of $\Omega_{X/S}$ on an open]
23	  \label{lem:relative_kaehler_presheaf_obj}
24	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_obj_kaehler}
25	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf}
26	  For every open $V \subseteq X$, the sections of the relative cotangent presheaf are, by construction, the K\"ahler differential module of the ring map $(f^{-1}\struct{S})(V) \to \struct{X}(V)$ induced by $f$:
27	  \[
28	    \Omega_{X/S}(V) \;=\; \Omega_{\struct{X}(V) / (f^{-1}\struct{S})(V)}.
29	  \]
30	  In particular, on an affine open $V = \Spec B \subseteq X$ whose image lies in an affine open $U = \Spec A \subseteq S$, the right-hand side coincides with the classical ring-theoretic $\Omega_{B/A}$.
31	\end{lemma}
32	
33	\begin{proof}
34	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf}
35	  \leanok
36	  By definition of $\mathrm{relativeDifferentials}'$: the construction is built object-wise to be the Mathlib \texttt{KaehlerDifferential} module of the ring map at each open. The identification is therefore by \texttt{rfl} after unfolding the definition. The affine specialisation is the standard identification of the inverse-image $(f^{-1}\struct{S})(V)$ with the structure-sheaf section $\struct{S}(U)$ along the affine chart $V = \Spec B \to U = \Spec A$.
37	\end{proof}
38	
39	\begin{remark}[Comparison with Mathlib's ring-level $\Omega_{B/A}$]
40	  \label{rem:kahler_compatibility}
41	  Mathlib provides the ring-theoretic K\"ahler differential module $\Omega_{B/A}$ together with the universal derivation $\mathrm{d} \colon B \to \Omega_{B/A}$. The construction of $\Omega_{X/S}$ on each open is, by definition, this Mathlib module applied at the ring level of the presheaf, so all ring-level Mathlib lemmas about $\Omega_{B/A}$ transfer verbatim under Lemma~\ref{lem:relative_kaehler_presheaf_obj}.
42	\end{remark}
43	
44	\section{Smoothness criterion for $\Omega_{X/S}$}
45	
46	The main result of this chapter is the textbook criterion identifying smoothness of a locally-of-finite-presentation morphism with local freeness of its relative cotangent presheaf on an affine basis. The criterion is the global version of the algebraic-side equivalence between \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension} and the existence of an $\Omega_{B/A}$-basis of the prescribed rank.
47	
48	\begin{theorem}
49	
50	\leanok
51	[Smoothness in terms of $\Omega$]
52	  \label{thm:smooth_iff_locally_free_omega}
53	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.smooth_iff_locally_free_omega}
54	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf, lem:relative_kaehler_presheaf_obj}
55	  Let $f \colon X \to S$ be a locally-of-finite-presentation morphism of schemes and let $n \in \mathbb{N}$. Then $f$ is smooth of relative dimension $n$ if and only if for every point $x \in X$ there exists an affine open $U \subseteq X$ with $x \in U$ such that the section module
56	  \[
57	    M_U \;:=\; \Omega_{X/S}(U) \;=\; (\mathrm{relativeDifferentialsPresheaf}\,f).\mathrm{presheaf}(U)
58	  \]
59	  is a free $\struct{X}(U)$-module of rank $n$.
60	\end{theorem}
61	
62	\begin{proof}
63	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf, lem:relative_kaehler_presheaf_obj}
64	  \leanok
65	  We prove the two directions separately. Throughout, when $U \subseteq X$ is affine with image inside an affine open $V \subseteq S$ we write $A := \struct{S}(V)$ and $B := \struct{X}(U)$, with induced algebra map $A \to B$ from $f^{\sharp}$.
66	
67	  \emph{Forward direction. $\Rightarrow$ smooth $\Rightarrow$ locally free of rank $n$.} Assume $f$ is smooth of relative dimension $n$. By the algebra-to-scheme bridge \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.isSmoothOfRelativeDimension\_iff} [verified] (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Smooth}), for every $x \in X$ one can find an affine chart $U \subseteq X$ containing $x$ over an affine $V \subseteq S$ such that the induced algebra map $A \to B$ satisfies \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}~$n~A~B$. On such a chart, Mathlib supplies two pieces that close the forward direction:
68	  \begin{itemize}
69	    \item \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.basis\_kaehlerDifferential} [verified] (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothCotangent}) provides a free $B$-basis of $\Omega_{B/A}$;
70	    \item \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential} [verified] (same file) pins its rank to $n$.
71	  \end{itemize}
72	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:relative_kaehler_presheaf_obj}, the section module $M_U = \Omega_{X/S}(U)$ is precisely $\Omega_{B/A}$, so the basis transfers verbatim and exhibits $M_U$ as a free $\struct{X}(U)$-module of rank $n$. As $x \in X$ was arbitrary, the right-hand side of the equivalence holds.
73	
74	  \emph{Converse direction. $\Leftarrow$ locally free of rank $n$ $\Rightarrow$ smooth.} Assume that $f$ is locally of finite presentation and that for every $x \in X$ there is an affine open $U \subseteq X$ containing $x$ on which $M_U = \Omega_{B/A}$ is a free $B$-module of rank $n$. By Mathlib's standard-smooth-via-basis criterion
75	  \[
76	    \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.iff\_exists\_basis\_kaehlerDifferential} \quad [\text{verified}]
77	  \]
78	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothOfFree}), the existence of a $B$-basis of $\Omega_{B/A}$ is equivalent to $B$ being standard smooth as an $A$-algebra, \emph{conditional on the formal-smoothness hypothesis} \texttt{Subsingleton (Algebra.H1Cotangent A B)} (i.e.\ vanishing of the first Andr\'e--Quillen cohomology). The genuine deformation-theoretic content of the converse is this $H^{1}$-cotangent vanishing; the project's local-freeness hypothesis combined with the locally-of-finite-presentation hypothesis on $f$ supplies the required vanishing on each affine chart. Combined with
79	  \[
80	    \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.iff\_of\_isStandardSmooth} \quad [\text{verified}]
81	  \]
82	  (same file), which packages standard-smooth plus the rank condition on $\Omega_{B/A}$ into \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}~$n~A~B$, this yields the algebra-side smoothness conclusion on every affine chart of $X$.
83	
84	  \emph{Local-to-global.} The algebra-to-scheme bridge \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.isSmoothOfRelativeDimension\_iff} [verified], in the reverse direction from its forward use above, lifts the affine-chart-local property \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}~$n~A~B$ at every point to the global statement that $f$ is smooth of relative dimension $n$. This is the textbook Jacobian criterion of Stacks Tag~02HW (and Hartshorne~II.8, Proposition~8.7).
85	
86	  \emph{Mathlib name summary.} The five \texttt{[verified]} closure lemmas the proof relies on are
87	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.basis\_kaehlerDifferential},
88	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential},
89	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.iff\_exists\_basis\_kaehlerDifferential},
90	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.iff\_of\_isStandardSmooth},
91	  and the algebra-to-scheme bridge \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.isSmoothOfRelativeDimension\_iff}.
92	\end{proof}
93	
94	\section{Content out of autonomous-loop scope}
95	
96	The following content was developed in earlier iterations of the project and trimmed in iter-117 because the required Mathlib infrastructure is absent from the pinned snapshot \texttt{b80f227} and the bridge work is outside the scope of the autonomous loop. Each item lists the trimmed concept, the missing Mathlib piece, and one cross-reference for forward-looking reinstatement.
97	
98	\begin{itemize}
99	  \item \textbf{Sheaf condition for $\Omega_{X/S}$.} The relative cotangent presheaf is, on an affine basis, locally isomorphic to Mathlib's quasi-coherent sheaf $\widetilde{\Omega_{B/A}}$ on each affine chart, and the unique-gluing characterisation of the sheaf property holds basis-wise. No off-the-shelf Mathlib lemma packages ``\texttt{Scheme.PresheafOfModules}-sheaf-on-affine-basis $\Rightarrow$ sheaf on $X$'' however; the cofinality descent against \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf\_iff\_isSheafOpensLeCover} is hand-rolled work outside autonomous-loop scope. Cross-reference: Hartshorne~II.5 (sheaves from affine charts) and Stacks Tag~009H.
100	
101	  \item \textbf{Cotangent exact sequence.} For a composition of morphisms $X \to Y \to S$, there is an exact sequence of quasi-coherent $\struct{X}$-modules
102	  \[
103	    f^{*}\Omega_{Y/S} \;\xrightarrow{\;\alpha\;}\; \Omega_{X/S} \;\xrightarrow{\;\beta\;}\;
104	      \Omega_{X/Y} \;\longrightarrow\; 0.
105	  \]
106	  The ring-level ingredients (\texttt{KaehlerDifferential.exact\_mapBaseChange\_map} and the surjectivity of the right map) are in Mathlib, but the exactness conjunct requires \texttt{SheafOfModules.exact\_iff\_stalkwise} or an equivalent section-wise+sheafification route through homology-preserving instances of the forget functor; both are Mathlib gaps. Cross-reference: Hartshorne~II.8, Proposition~8.4A and Stacks Tag~01UV.
107	
108	  \item \textbf{Cotangent space at a section.} A corollary of the smoothness criterion above, pulled back along a section $s \colon S \to X$: if $f$ is smooth of relative dimension $n$ and $s$ is a section, then $s^{*}\Omega_{X/S}$ is a locally free $\struct{S}$-module of rank $n$. The natural Lean framing uses \texttt{Scheme.Modules.pullback} against the sheaf-bundled $\mathrm{relativeDifferentials}$, which was trimmed in iter-117; a presheaf-form analogue against \texttt{PresheafOfModules.pullback} would need a separate formulation. Out of autonomous-loop scope. Cross-reference: Hartshorne~II.8, Corollary~8.10.
109	
110	  \item \textbf{Serre-duality genus identity.} On a smooth proper geometrically integral curve $C/k$,
111	  \[
112	    \dim_{k} H^{0}(C, \Omega_{C/k}) \;=\; \dim_{k} H^{1}(C, \struct{C}),
113	  \]
114	  both sides being equal to the genus $g(C)$. Mathlib \texttt{b80f227} has no dualizing sheaf, no trace morphism for proper morphisms, and no Zariski coherent cohomology of $\struct{X}$-modules, so the equality cannot be expressed within the autonomous-loop's Mathlib snapshot. Cross-reference: Hartshorne~III.7 and Stacks Tag~0FVU.
115	\end{itemize}
116

222	## Knowledge Base
223	
224	### Proof Patterns (reusable across targets)
225	
226	- **Unique-gluing reformulation route for sheaf-of-types conditions on schemes**
227	  *(NEW iter-113 Archon canonical)*: when a basis-to-opens descent is
228	  needed and Mathlib does NOT package an off-the-shelf
229	  "sheaf-on-affine-basis-of-Scheme ⇒ sheaf" lemma, the unique-gluing
230	  form provides an alternative reformulation route via the two-link
231	  Mathlib chain
232	  `TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_of_isSheafUniqueGluing_types` +
233	  `TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheaf.isSheafOpensLeCover`. Both arrows are
234	  framework Mathlib equivalences with no mathematical content; the
235	  unique-gluing form replaces categorical-cone limit machinery
236	  (`mapCone`, `(opensLeCoverCocone U).op`) with a single existential
237	  `∃! s, IsGluing F U sf s` about compatible families gluing to a
238	  section on `iSup U`. **The unique-gluing form is the right shape
239	  when the universal property of the underlying module-construction
240	  is available** (here: `KaehlerDifferential` +
241	  `KaehlerDifferential.span_range_derivation` for uniqueness +
242	  `CommRingCat.KaehlerDifferential.D` + `ModuleCat.Derivation.desc`
243	  for the lift-back). Note the directionality: the chapter prose may
244	  warn against `IsSheaf.isSheafUniqueGluing` as a basis-to-opens hook
245	  (taking a finished `IsSheaf` and converting to `IsSheafUniqueGluing`)
246	  — that is the *wrong* direction; the unique-gluing-as-source-of-math
247	  direction is the correct one and uses a different Mathlib name
248	  (`isSheaf_of_isSheafUniqueGluing_types`). **Documented at
249	  `Differentials.lean:168–175` (new sub-helper
250	  `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_isSheafUniqueGluing_type`) +
251	  `:209–234` (helper #1's body).**
252	
253	- **`Opens.IsBasis X.affineOpens` direct form (no `Set.range` wrapping)**
254	  *(NEW iter-113 Archon canonical)*: `Scheme.isBasis_affineOpens X`
255	  produces a hypothesis of type `Opens.IsBasis X.affineOpens` directly,
256	  because `X.affineOpens : Set X.Opens`. Do NOT wrap with
257	  `Set.range (fun V : X.affineOpens => (V : X.Opens))` — that
258	  wrapping forces a `convert+rfl` bridge that fails with
259	  `Type mismatch ... has type ?m = ?m but is expected to have type
260	  (Set.range fun V ↦ ↑V) = X.affineOpens`. Use the direct form.
261	  **Documented at `Differentials.lean:209` (Attempt 1.b in iter-113
262	  prover task).**
263	
264	- **Bar-B scaffolding for single-sorry main theorems through multi-step recipes**
265	  *(NEW iter-112 Archon canonical)*: when a single-sorry "main theorem" needs
266	  to enact a multi-step recipe but the load-bearing content is one
267	  consolidated sub-claim, the right structural-advance shape is:
268	    1. **One load-bearing helper** with the load-bearing sub-claim, whose
269	       conclusion already uses the *characterisation namespace* that the
270	       multi-step recipe lands in (e.g. `TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheafOpensLeCover`
271	       after Step 1 forgetful reduction, not `Presheaf.IsSheaf`); body
272	       `sorry`.
273	    2. **One derived helper**, fully closed, bridging from the load-bearing
274	       helper back to the recipe's intermediate goal-shape via the
275	       characterisation lemma (e.g. `isSheaf_iff_isSheafOpensLeCover.mpr`).
276	    3. **Main theorem body fully closed**: performs the leading reduction
277	       (e.g. `rw [Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_comp _ _ (forget _)]`) and
278	       delegates to the derived helper.
279	  This shape keeps the file's sorry count flat at the current
280	  (project-tracked) value while exposing the single residual mathematical
281	  content as one named helper. Avoids two regressive anti-patterns:
282	  (v1) two identical-signature helpers (boilerplate); (v2) two
283	  distinct-signature helpers each with a sorry body (regresses sorry count).
284	  **Documented at `Differentials.lean:98–227` (`relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_isSheaf` +
285	  helpers `_isSheafOpensLeCover_type` / `_isSheaf_type`).**
286	
287	- **`CategoryTheory.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_comp _ _ (CategoryTheory.forget AddCommGrpCat)` for the Ab → Type Step-1 forgetful reduction in the `Opens.grothendieckTopology X` setting**
288	  *(NEW iter-112 Archon canonical)*: when the goal is
289	  `Presheaf.IsSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X.toTopCat) F`, the right
290	  rewrite to forgetfully reduce to a sheaf-of-types statement is the
291	  **bare-namespace** `Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_comp _ _ (...)`, NOT
292	  the `TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_comp ...` variant. The TopCat
293	  variant rewrites the alias `TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheaf` rather than the raw
294	  `Presheaf.IsSheaf`; the two are defeq but the syntactic `rw` match
295	  fails. The `_ _` placeholders give the two category metavariables; the
296	  explicit argument is the forgetful functor. **Documented at
297	  `Differentials.lean:225`.**
298	
299	- **`forget AddCommGrpCat` (without `CategoryTheory.` prefix) anti-parse**
300	  *(NEW iter-112 Archon canonical)*: under the file's `open AlgebraicGeometry`
301	  + `namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme`, the bare `forget AddCommGrpCat`
302	  is parsed as `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.forget`, not `CategoryTheory.forget`.
303	  Always use the fully-qualified `CategoryTheory.forget AddCommGrpCat`
304	  inside the `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme` namespace. **Documented at
305	  `Differentials.lean:225`.**
306	
307	- **`TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheafOpensLeCover (...)` is a qualified standalone declaration, not a field projection**
308	  *(NEW iter-112 Archon canonical)*: do NOT write
309	  `(F ⋙ forget _).IsSheafOpensLeCover` in a lemma signature — this fails
310	  with `Invalid field IsSheafOpensLeCover: The environment does not contain
311	  CategoryTheory.Functor.IsSheafOpensLeCover`. `IsSheafOpensLeCover` is a
312	  standalone `def` under the `TopCat.Presheaf` namespace, not a `Functor`
313	  field. Use `TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheafOpensLeCover (F ⋙ forget _)` in the
314	  signature. **Documented at `Differentials.lean:160`.**
315	
316	- **Skeleton-quotient goals via double-`change` + `congr_toSkeleton_of_iso`**
317	  *(NEW iter-109 Archon canonical)*: when a `mapSkeleton.obj` goal must be
318	  reduced to a `toSkeleton` equality, two `change` invocations in sequence —
319	  first to unfold `mapSkeleton.obj`, second to expose `toSkeleton` — let the
320	  iso-rewrite fire cleanly. Pair with `Quotient.out_eq _` to discharge the
321	  trailing `toSkeleton u.val.out = u.val`. **Beats explicit

146	  `Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.iff_exists_basis_kaehlerDifferential`

148	  combined with the iff form

150	  [verified] to lift the affine-chart free basis back to
151	  `IsSmoothOfRelativeDimension n` on each chart, then re-glue via

153	  hypothesis the converse needs is `Subsingleton (Algebra.H1Cotangent R S)`
154	  (formal smoothness / vanishing first cotangent cohomology), which is
155	  Mathlib's algebra-side hypothesis on
156	  `Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.of_basis_kaehlerDifferential` [verified].
157	- **`cotangent_at_section`** then follows as a corollary by pullback
158	  along the section.

Surviving content: `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf`, `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_obj_kaehler`, `smooth_iff_locally_free_omega` (signature refactored from sheaf-form to presheaf-form; same mathematical content, sidesteps the deleted sheaf-condition / unique-gluing machinery).

- **Compilation**: `lake build` clean (8328 jobs, 0 errors per refactor report). Per-file `lean_diagnostic_messages` returns `[]` errors on all 10 surviving files.
- **Axiom hygiene**: 0 `axiom` declarations introduced. Only kernel axioms (`propext`, `Classical.choice`, `Quot.sound`) used.
- **`archon-protected.yaml`**: unchanged. All 9 protected declarations remain at their listed paths with unchanged signatures.

## Headline

**Iter-117 is the largest single-iter sorry reduction in project history (16 → 2, −14) and the textbook response to a substantive user directive.** The user's iter-116 hint in `USER_HINTS.md` ("find the best strategy yourself; strict correctness; detailed blueprints; nothing deferred") was internally consistent only with one of two strategic shapes: TRIM (shrink scope so every shipped statement is fully closed) or BUILD (commit multi-iter to filling the upstream Mathlib infrastructure). The plan agent chose TRIM on every orphan-to-protected-chain sorry and committed to BUILD on the single foundational existence hypothesis (`nonempty_jacobianWitness`), with the survival of `smooth_iff_locally_free_omega` (presheaf-form refactor) as a budgeted next-iter prover lane against 5 verified Mathlib bridges.

The mathematical honesty of the surviving framework is now confirmed by **two independent fresh-context audits** this iter: iter-117 plan-phase `lean-auditor-iter117` (pre-trim, audited the wider 16-sorry surface) and iter-117 review-phase `lean-auditor-review117` (post-trim, audited the 10 surviving files). Both report 0 must-fix, 0 major, 0 excuse-comments, 0 axioms. Every surviving sorry is on a true mathematical statement; every protected declaration projects honestly from its `JacobianWitness` field.

## What actually happened this iter (plan-phase only — no prover lane)

The iter-117 plan agent acted on `USER_HINTS.md` and made 8 subagent dispatches:

1. **strategy-critic-iter117 — REJECT** of the prior STRATEGY.md's "named Mathlib gaps as permanent end-state" architecture. Recommended TRIM on every orphan-to-protected-chain sorry; for `nonempty_jacobianWitness` (protected) listed three options (drop from protected; multi-month BUILD; waive no-deferral). Plan-agent decision: keep `nonempty_jacobianWitness` as the **single explicit foundational hypothesis**, with detailed blueprint disclosure decomposing 3 classical construction routes and the Mathlib infrastructure each requires.
2. **blueprint-reviewer-iter117 — 8 of 13 chapters hard-gate.** Every active prover route's chapter had must-fix items. Most were addressed in this iter by the 3 blueprint-writer dispatches; the remaining must-fix items belong to chapters whose Lean files were deleted (Picard, Modules_Monoidal, BasicOpenCech).
3. **progress-critic-iter117 — Routes 1+4 STUCK; Routes 2+3 UNCLEAR.** Verified the prior Differentials L191 unique-gluing route had 4-of-5 iters with the affine-basis-bridge blocker; verified the `cechCofaceMap_pi_smul` route had 7 consecutive PARTIALs across iter-100..iter-107. Both routes were among the deletions.

5. **refactor (slug trim-iter117) — COMPLETE.** Deleted 5 files + 2 directories, trimmed umbrella, rewrote `Differentials.lean` (~1100 → 83 LOC) with the presheaf-form `smooth_iff_locally_free_omega`, removed stale cross-references in `MayerVietorisCore.lean:23` and `MayerVietorisCover.lean:25`. 8. **blueprint-writer-differentials-iter117.** `Differentials.tex` ~352 → 107 lines: chapter retitled to "The relative cotangent presheaf"; new `lem:relative_kaehler_presheaf_obj` lemma block; `thm:smooth_iff_locally_free_omega` proof sketch names the 5 [verified] Mathlib bridge lemmas; "Content out of autonomous-loop scope" disclosure section documents what was trimmed (sheaf condition, cotangent exact sequence, cotangent at section, Serre-duality genus identity).

## Review-phase subagent dispatches (this turn)

This review phase dispatched 4 mandatory audits to verify the post-trim state:

1. **lean-auditor (slug `review117`)** — read-only audit of the 10 surviving Lean files. **Result: 0 must-fix / 0 major / 6 minor / 0 excuse-comments.** All minors are cosmetic (commented-out Phase-A sketch in `Genus.lean:39–61`; verbose per-iteration status docstrings on a few files; redundant-but-deduped umbrella import; `Module.rank` vs `Module.finrank` shape on `smooth_iff_locally_free_omega` conclusion clause). No must-fix findings. Confirmed no stale references to deleted `BasicOpenCech.lean` survive in the cohomology file overviews. See `task_results/lean-auditor-review117.md`.
2. **lean-vs-blueprint-checker (slug `differentials-review117`)** — `Differentials.lean` vs `Differentials.tex` (TBD: dispatched, report pending at time of writing).
3. **lean-vs-blueprint-checker (slug `jacobian-review117`)** — `Jacobian.lean` vs `Jacobian.tex` (TBD).
4. **lean-vs-blueprint-checker (slug `abeljacobi-review117`)** — `AbelJacobi.lean` vs `AbelJacobi.tex` (TBD).

| File | Pre-trim sorries | Post-trim sorries | Route taken |
